label: Bot
alert_text: TCP flow on port 8080/HTTP-Proxy Volume: 7 packets total (3 forward / 4 backward), 455 bytes total (326 B sent, 129 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.4x (client-dominant). Duration: 9.3 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1.6 ms, std 3.3 ms, max 8.3 ms. Forward IAT: mean 0.3 ms, max 0.6 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 326 B, std 188.2 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 112 B, std 53.8 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow Duration: 9309.0 (unusually low, z=-9.9); Flow IAT Mean: 1551.5 (unusually low, z=-9.9).

output.threat_description: Short-lived TCP session to an HTTP-Proxy on port 8080 from a client using a non-standard TCP stack (init window 8192 B), terminated by RST after only 7 packets. The client-dominant byte ratio with a single PSH followed by an abrupt reset is characteristic of botnet C2 beaconing or proxy-tunneling attempts where the bot posts a small payload and tears the connection down to evade flow-based detection.
output.rationale: Behavioral fingerprint (custom stack, micro-burst, abnormal teardown, proxy port) is consistent with malware check-in traffic; if confirmed, the host is already compromised and under remote control.
mitigation_steps: Isolate the source host from the network and trigger a forensic acquisition of memory and persistence artifacts.
Block egress traffic to the destination IP and port 8080 at the perimeter and add the proxy endpoint to your threat-intel feed.
Hunt for additional internal hosts contacting the same destination to identify the full botnet footprint.

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK-only probe to an HTTP-Proxy port with no server response and a zero-byte payload. The non-standard initial window (2052 B) and the lone ACK without SYN handshake indicate a mid-flow capture of a bot probing whether its proxy/C2 channel is reachable, a typical liveness-check pattern used by botnet implants.
output.rationale: No payload was exchanged and the server did not respond, limiting immediate impact, but the fingerprint matches automated bot reconnaissance rather than legitimate user activity.
mitigation_steps: Correlate the source IP with other 8080 probes over time to detect a beacon cadence.
Enrich the destination IP against threat intelligence and block at the egress firewall if attributed to known C2 infrastructure.
Run an EDR scan on the source host to look for unauthorized network clients or scheduled callbacks.

output.threat_description: Identical bot-probe pattern: an ACK-only two-packet flow to port 8080 with zero payload and no server reply. The unusually small initial window (256 B) is consistent with an embedded or stripped-down malware TCP stack rather than a real browser, suggesting another instance of botnet C2 reachability testing.
output.rationale: Probe-only behavior with no data exchanged keeps impact low, but the recurring fingerprint across multiple flows points to persistent compromise on the source host.
mitigation_steps: Quarantine the source endpoint pending malware sweep and uninstall of unauthorized software.
Add an IDS signature for ACK-only HTTP-Proxy probes from internal subnets toward external IPs.
Block the destination IP at the egress firewall until benign attribution is confirmed.

label: DoS attacks-Hulk
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 2 packets total (2 forward / 0 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 18.6 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 18.6 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 18.6 ms. Forward IAT: mean 18.6 ms, max 18.6 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1. TCP window: client init window 225 B (unknown OS); server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK-only flow to HTTP/80 with no SYN handshake and no payload, captured mid-stream during an HTTP Unbearable Load King (HULK) flood. HULK generates large volumes of randomized GETs to bypass server-side caching; this flow is the connection-teardown fragment of one such request among many being sprayed at the web server.
output.rationale: A single flow looks benign, but in HULK the damage comes from thousands of these per second exhausting worker threads and connection pools; aggregate impact on the web tier is significant.
mitigation_steps: Enable rate-limiting and connection-per-IP caps at the reverse proxy or WAF in front of the web server.
Block the offending source IP/ASN and apply geo-fencing if attacker geography is narrow.
Tune the web server (worker timeouts, keep-alive limits) and front it with a CDN or scrubbing service to absorb L7 floods.

Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Another fragment of a HULK-style application-layer DoS: an ACK-only HTTP/80 micro-flow from the same fingerprint (init window 225 B, min segment 32 B) with no payload. Repetition of this exact signature confirms an automated flood generator rather than legitimate browsing.
output.rationale: Volumetric L7 DoS impacts service availability and degrades legitimate user experience; persistence of the same fingerprint indicates an ongoing attack campaign.
mitigation_steps: Drop traffic matching the attacker fingerprint (TCP window 225, ACK-only HTTP flows) at the edge.
Activate upstream DDoS mitigation/scrubbing and switch the site behind a CDN with anti-bot challenges.
Scale out web workers and shorten idle-connection timeouts to free resources held by the flood.

Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Third recurrence of the same HULK fingerprint against HTTP/80, again a two-packet ACK-only zero-byte flow. The consistent custom TCP-stack fingerprint across flows points to a single attack tool generating massive request volume against the web application.
output.rationale: Confirmed sustained L7 flood from a consistent source profile; cumulative load threatens web service availability.
mitigation_steps: Apply WAF rules to challenge or block requests lacking valid HTTP semantics or matching the HULK URL/header pattern.
Rate-limit per source IP at the load balancer and null-route the attacker subnet upstream if it persists.
Notify your upstream provider and engage DDoS mitigation services if the attack scales beyond local capacity.

label: DoS attacks-SlowHTTPTest
alert_text: TCP flow on port 21/FTP Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS). Minimum forward segment size: 40 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 666666.6667 (>p99, z=6431730.6); Flow IAT Mean: 3.0 (unusually low, z=-3.8).

output.threat_description: A burst on TCP/21 (FTP) showing a single PSH packet with zero payload and an extraordinary packet rate (~666k pkts/s) calculated from a sub-millisecond duration. The pattern matches slowhttptest-style connection-holding behavior misdirected at FTP, where the attacker opens many concurrent sockets and dribbles minimal data to exhaust the daemon's connection pool.
output.rationale: FTP daemons typically have low concurrent-connection ceilings; slow-resource exhaustion against port 21 can knock the service offline quickly and may also mask credential brute force underneath.
mitigation_steps: Enforce per-source concurrent-connection limits on the FTP service and reduce idle/header read timeouts.
Block the source IP at the perimeter and inspect the FTP server logs for accompanying brute-force attempts.
Migrate to SFTP/FTPS behind a reverse proxy or VPN, removing direct public exposure of port 21.

output.threat_description: Repeat slowhttptest-style fragment on FTP/21: a sub-millisecond PSH-only flow with no payload and a packet rate over 600k/s, again pointing to a connection-exhaustion tool aimed at the FTP daemon.
output.rationale: Recurring identical fingerprint confirms an automated slow-resource attack campaign; FTP services are particularly fragile under this pressure.
mitigation_steps: Throttle new connections per source on TCP/21 at the firewall and enforce strict timeout limits in the FTP server config.
Blacklist the originating IP/ASN and review FTP logs for related authentication abuse.
Front the FTP service with a bastion or remove public exposure entirely.

output.threat_description: Same slowhttptest connection-holding signature against FTP/21 with packet rate ~90k/s. The PSH-only zero-byte payload combined with a custom client TCP stack (init window 26883 B) is the hallmark of the slowhttptest tool emitting micro-requests to keep sockets pinned.
output.rationale: Persistent slow-resource attack against an FTP service that has limited slot capacity; sustained activity will exhaust available worker connections.
mitigation_steps: Apply IP-based rate limits and slot quotas at the FTP server and at the edge firewall.
Block the originating subnet and harden the daemon (e.g., MaxClients, MaxClientsPerIP) against partial-request abuse.
Reduce attack surface by restricting port 21 to authenticated VPN clients only.

label: SQL Injection
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 8 packets total (4 forward / 4 backward), 1055 bytes total (660 B sent, 395 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.6x (balanced). Duration: 5.01 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 715.8 ms, std 1867.6 ms, max 4950.8 ms. Forward IAT: mean 19.8 ms, max 56.2 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 660 B, std 330.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 395 B, std 197.5 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 221 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: A balanced HTTP/80 conversation lasting ~5 seconds with a small payload (660 B sent, 395 B returned) and final RST teardown, originating from a client with a non-standard TCP stack (init window 8192 B, custom tool). The sub-second forward IATs followed by a multi-second idle and reset are consistent with a time-based blind SQL injection probe from sqlmap or similar, where the attacker injects sleep/benchmark payloads and waits for the server's delayed response to confirm exploitability.
output.rationale: SQL injection enables direct database read/write and potential authentication bypass or RCE; the timing pattern indicates active exploitation testing, not generic scanning.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the WAF and enable signature/anomaly rules for SQLi payload patterns (UNION, SLEEP, BENCHMARK, time-based).
Review web and database logs for the targeted endpoint and verify parameterized queries are in use; patch any vulnerable parameter immediately.
Rotate any credentials that may have been exposed and audit the database for unauthorized reads or schema enumeration.

label: SQL Injection

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK-only probe to HTTP/80 with zero payload and very high computed packet rate. Likely the residual ACK/teardown segment of a scripted SQLi scanner that completed (or aborted) an injection attempt on another connection in the same campaign.
output.rationale: On its own this flow carries no payload, but the SQLi-labeled context and scripted fingerprint indicate a broader injection campaign requiring response.
mitigation_steps: Correlate the source IP with other HTTP/80 flows for full request reconstruction and WAF inspection.
Block the source IP and add WAF signatures for SQLi heuristics on the affected endpoints.
Audit web-app and DB logs for injection attempts against vulnerable parameters.

label: Brute Force -Web
alert_text: UDP flow on port 500 Volume: 6 packets total (6 forward / 0 backward), 3000 bytes total (3000 B sent, 0 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.0x (client-dominant). Duration: 1.5 min. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 17895.9 ms, std 15345.2 ms, max 41990.5 ms. Forward IAT: mean 17895.9 ms, max 41990.5 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 500 B, max 500 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: none set. TCP window: server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 8 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow IAT Mean: 17895933.8 (unusually low, z=-11.4).

output.threat_description: Six fixed-size 500-byte UDP packets to port 500 over ~90 seconds with no replies. Port 500 carries IKE (IPsec) and the periodic identical-size unsolicited datagrams are characteristic of IKE aggressive-mode brute force or PSK enumeration against a VPN gateway, where the attacker iterates identities/PSKs looking for a vulnerable responder.
output.rationale: Successful VPN PSK compromise yields full network ingress; even unanswered IKE probes indicate active enumeration of the VPN concentrator.
mitigation_steps: Disable IKE aggressive mode on the VPN gateway and require certificate-based auth or strong unique PSKs.
Rate-limit and geo-filter inbound UDP/500 and UDP/4500, and block the source IP at the edge.
Enable IKE logging and alert on repeated identity-mismatch or auth-failure events from any single source.

label: SQL Injection
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 8 packets total (4 forward / 4 backward), 923 bytes total (535 B sent, 388 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.73x (balanced). Duration: 5.01 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 715.8 ms, std 1865.0 ms, max 4944.9 ms. Forward IAT: mean 21.8 ms, max 62.1 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 535 B, std 267.5 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 388 B, std 194.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: HTTP/80 session of ~5 seconds with balanced bytes (535 B / 388 B), terminated by RST after a single PSH, from a non-standard TCP stack (init window 8192 B). The cadence — quick request bursts followed by multi-second waits — matches automated time-based blind SQL injection probing where the tool measures server response delay to infer injection success.
output.rationale: Time-based SQLi reliably extracts data even with no visible error output; successful exploitation grants database-level access to the application.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the WAF and enable strict SQLi rule sets (timing, boolean, union, error-based).
Audit the targeted application endpoint and convert dynamic queries to parameterized statements; deploy emergency patches if vulnerable.
Review database query logs for SLEEP/BENCHMARK/WAITFOR patterns and rotate any credentials potentially exposed.

Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Three sub-millisecond ACK-only packets to HTTP/80 with no payload and no server response — a fingerprint of automated XSS-payload spraying tools where the scripted client opens many short-lived sockets to inject crafted strings into reflected parameters. This particular flow captures connection-teardown fragments of the wider scan.
output.rationale: No payload visible in this flow, but the fingerprint and label indicate an automated XSS injection campaign in progress against the web application.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the WAF and enable reflected/stored XSS signature rules.
Verify output encoding and Content-Security-Policy headers on the targeted endpoints.
Hunt web logs for the source IP's full request set to confirm targeted parameters and payloads.

label: Brute Force -Web
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 8 packets total (5 forward / 3 backward), 1010 bytes total (646 B sent, 364 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.56x (balanced). Duration: 5.01 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 715.1 ms, std 1862.1 ms, max 4937.7 ms. Forward IAT: mean 1251.4 ms, max 4937.7 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 646 B, std 288.9 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 364 B, std 210.2 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 221 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: Five-second HTTP/80 session of 8 packets with balanced traffic and RST teardown, originating from a non-standard TCP stack (init window 8192 B). The cadence — multi-second forward IATs between small request bursts — is characteristic of a credential brute-force tool (e.g., Hydra, Burp Intruder) submitting login form attempts with throttling to evade simple rate limits.
output.rationale: Successful credential compromise grants direct application access; the pattern is consistent with active authentication abuse rather than reconnaissance.
mitigation_steps: Enforce account lockout, CAPTCHA, and per-IP rate limiting on the login endpoint, and block the source IP at the WAF.
Enable MFA on all exposed accounts and review authentication logs for successful logins from the source IP.
Rotate credentials for any account that authenticated from the attacker IP during the attack window.

label: Brute Force -Web

Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 17895.9 ms, std 15345.2 ms, max 41990.4 ms. Forward IAT: mean 17895.9 ms, max 41990.4 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 500 B, max 500 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: none set. TCP window: server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 8 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow IAT Mean: 17895921.2 (unusually low, z=-11.7).

output.threat_description: Repeated IKE/IPsec enumeration pattern: six 500-byte UDP/500 packets over ~90 seconds with no response, consistent with PSK or identity brute force against the VPN gateway. The fixed-size payload and slow cadence are designed to look like normal IKE retries while iterating candidate secrets.
output.rationale: Persistent IKE brute force is a path to full network ingress via VPN; unmitigated, weak PSKs can be cracked offline once a handshake is observed.
mitigation_steps: Disable IKE aggressive mode and migrate to certificate or EAP-based authentication on the VPN.
Block the source IP and rate-limit UDP/500 and UDP/4500 at the perimeter.
Audit current PSKs for entropy and rotate any that are short or shared across users.

label: Brute Force -XSS
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 307 packets total (203 forward / 104 backward), 245986 bytes total (56083 B sent, 189903 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 3.39x (server-dominant). Duration: 57.1 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 186.6 ms, std 363.1 ms, max 1019.5 ms. Forward IAT: mean 282.7 ms, max 1019.5 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 680 B, std 291.7 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 1935 B, std 320.4 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 1281 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: A 57-second HTTP/80 conversation of 307 packets with strongly server-dominant byte ratio (3.39x, ~246 KB total) from a non-standard TCP stack (init window 8192 B). The volume of returned content combined with sustained forward POST/GET bursts is consistent with an active XSS fuzzing/brute-force campaign in which the attacker submits many crafted payloads and harvests reflected responses to identify exploitable injection points.
output.rationale: Substantial back-and-forth at scale indicates the attacker is past reconnaissance and actively probing for working XSS payloads — high likelihood of finding a reflected or stored vector.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the WAF and enable XSS payload signatures plus anomaly scoring for input fuzzing.
Verify CSP, HttpOnly cookies, and output encoding on all user-input rendering paths.
Review web logs for the source IP, identify which parameters reflected payloads, and patch the inputs immediately.

output.rationale: Single fragment carries no payload, but is a piece of a broader XSS brute-force campaign captured in adjacent flows.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the WAF and aggregate its requests for full payload reconstruction.

label: Infilteration
alert_text: UDP flow on port 53/DNS Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 80 bytes total (32 B sent, 48 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 1.5x (balanced). Duration: 0.3 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.3 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.3 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 32 B, max 32 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 48 B, max 48 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: none set. TCP window: server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 8 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow Pkts/s: 7604.562738 (>p95, z=2.7).

output.threat_description: Single DNS query/response pair on UDP/53 with high packet rate when aggregated. While individually unremarkable, in an infiltration context this pattern is consistent with DNS-based C2 or low-and-slow DNS tunneling, where compromised hosts encode small command/exfil chunks inside frequent DNS lookups to attacker-controlled domains.
output.rationale: DNS tunneling and DNS C2 commonly bypass perimeter controls; even minimal DNS volume can carry covert channels enabling persistent attacker access.
mitigation_steps: Force all internal DNS through inspecting resolvers and block direct outbound UDP/53 from endpoints.
Enable DNS query analytics looking for high-entropy subdomains, long names, or unusual TXT/NULL record volume to the destination.
Block the queried domain/IP if not whitelisted and run EDR on the source host for known infostealer/RAT indicators.

label: Infilteration
alert_text: TCP flow on port 53589 Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.1 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.1 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.1 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1, URGx1. TCP window: client init window 946 B (unknown OS); server init window 256 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Two-packet flow on the ephemeral TCP port 53589 carrying ACK with URG flag set and no payload. The URG flag is rarely used by modern applications and is a recognized covert-signaling and IDS-evasion technique, here suggesting a malware-issued out-of-band signal between an infected host and an attacker-controlled service.
output.rationale: URG-flag abuse on a non-standard port between internal and external endpoints is a strong indicator of malware C2 signaling, not benign traffic.
mitigation_steps: Isolate the source host and run a full EDR / memory acquisition for malware triage.
Block the destination IP and port at the firewall and add IDS rules flagging URG-flag-only segments on non-standard ports.
Hunt across the environment for other hosts contacting the same external endpoint.

label: Infilteration
alert_text: UDP flow on port 53/DNS Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 88 bytes total (36 B sent, 52 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 1.44x (balanced). Duration: 1.1 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1.1 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 1.1 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 36 B, max 36 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 52 B, max 52 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: none set. TCP window: server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 8 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: Another small DNS query/response pair on UDP/53. In an infiltration context this fits beaconing or staged exfiltration where the implant uses periodic resolver lookups as a covert channel for command retrieval or data leakage.
output.rationale: Low immediate volume keeps single-flow impact modest, but consistent low-rate DNS to suspicious destinations is a hallmark of stealthy C2 that should not be dismissed.
mitigation_steps: Route all client DNS through inspecting resolvers and enable telemetry for entropy/volume anomalies.
Block traffic to the queried domain if not on an approved allow-list and add it to threat intel.
Investigate the source host with EDR for the implant generating the lookups.

label: DoS attacks-GoldenEye
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 8 packets total (5 forward / 3 backward), 1126 bytes total (464 B sent, 662 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 1.43x (balanced). Duration: 6.81 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 972.7 ms, std 2545.3 ms, max 6744.7 ms. Forward IAT: mean 1702.3 ms, max 6745.1 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 464 B, std 207.5 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 662 B, std 382.2 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: A nearly 7-second HTTP/80 exchange of 8 packets with balanced byte ratio and a single PSH, from a client using the custom TCP stack fingerprint (init window 26883 B) seen across GoldenEye attacks. GoldenEye is a layer-7 HTTP DoS that opens few connections, asks for no-cache content, and keeps sockets alive via Keep-Alive to exhaust server worker pools — exactly matching the long-duration low-packet shape here.
output.rationale: GoldenEye-style L7 floods reliably exhaust Apache/nginx worker capacity; even small per-flow footprints aggregate into severe availability impact.
mitigation_steps: Limit concurrent connections per source and shorten Keep-Alive timeouts at the web server / load balancer.
Block the source IP and add WAF rules for GoldenEye signatures (no-cache, randomized URI parameters, suspicious User-Agent).
Front the application with a CDN or DDoS scrubber capable of L7 challenge-response for suspect clients.

label: DoS attacks-GoldenEye
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 8 packets total (4 forward / 4 backward), 990 bytes total (328 B sent, 662 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 2.02x (server-dominant). Duration: 11.46 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1636.4 ms, std 2825.2 ms, max 6449.5 ms. Forward IAT: mean 2150.3 ms, max 6449.8 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 328 B, std 164.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 662 B, std 331.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: Another GoldenEye L7 DoS flow: 8 packets over 11 seconds against HTTP/80, server-dominant bytes, single PSH, and the recurring init window 26883 B fingerprint. The long flow duration with sparse, evenly spaced packets is GoldenEye holding the connection open while issuing cache-busting requests.
output.rationale: Sustained connection-holding behavior at scale degrades web service availability; consistent fingerprint confirms an automated attack tool.
mitigation_steps: Block the offending source IP and apply per-IP concurrent-connection caps on the web tier.
Tune web server timeouts (KeepAliveTimeout, RequestReadTimeout) to reclaim resources from slow clients.
Engage upstream DDoS protection if the attack continues from multiple sources.

label: DoS attacks-GoldenEye
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 2 packets total (2 forward / 0 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 1.8 s. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1803.9 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 1803.9 ms. Forward IAT: mean 1803.9 ms, max 1803.9 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1. TCP window: client init window 226 B (unknown OS); server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK-only fragment on HTTP/80 spanning 1.8 seconds with no payload, captured mid-stream from a GoldenEye flood. The long IAT for an ACK-only flow reflects the tool deliberately keeping idle connections open to occupy server worker slots.
output.rationale: Single fragment has limited direct impact, but its long-idle shape confirms the connection-holding component of the layer-7 DoS campaign.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP and reduce server-side idle-connection timeouts.
Add WAF rate-limiting on /endpoint patterns targeted by GoldenEye.
Activate DDoS mitigation upstream if many such sources appear simultaneously.

label: DoS attacks-Slowloris
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 18 packets total (15 forward / 3 backward), 2530 bytes total (2530 B sent, 0 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.0x (client-dominant). Duration: 1.8 min. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 6375.4 ms, std 13533.5 ms, max 53247.3 ms. Forward IAT: mean 7741.6 ms, max 53247.3 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 230 B, std 105.3 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS). Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: Eighteen-packet HTTP/80 flow stretched over ~1.8 minutes with extreme inter-arrival gaps (mean 6.4 s, max 53 s) and zero backward bytes. This is the textbook Slowloris signature: the attacker dribbles partial HTTP headers very slowly so the server keeps the connection open waiting for the full request, eventually exhausting its worker pool.
output.rationale: Slowloris reliably DoS's threaded web servers (Apache prefork) with minimal bandwidth; a successful campaign takes down public-facing HTTP services with very few attacking hosts.
mitigation_steps: Enable mod_reqtimeout / RequestReadTimeout on Apache or migrate to an event-based server (nginx) less susceptible to slow headers.
Block the source IP and enforce minimum data-rate thresholds at the WAF / load balancer.
Cap concurrent connections per source IP and front the site with a reverse proxy that buffers full requests before passing them upstream.

label: DoS attacks-Slowloris
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 4 packets total (2 forward / 2 backward), 16 bytes total (16 B sent, 0 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.0x (client-dominant). Duration: 1.7 min. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 33337.1 ms, std 57741.6 ms, max 100011.4 ms. Forward IAT: mean 100011.4 ms, max 100011.4 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 8 B, max 8 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: SYNx1, ACKx1. TCP window: client init window 211 B (unknown OS); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B.

output.threat_description: Four-packet HTTP/80 flow over ~1.7 minutes with the SYN flag observed and only 16 bytes total sent, no data returned. The pattern — a fresh connection established and then held open with trickle data and ~100-second pauses — is consistent with Slowloris worker-hold behavior using minimum-viable activity to keep the socket alive.
output.rationale: Long-held minimal-data sockets are the operative mechanism of Slowloris availability attacks; sustained across many connections this exhausts server capacity.
mitigation_steps: Set strict header/body read timeouts on the web server and enforce minimum bytes-per-second per connection.
Block the source IP and add IDS rules for HTTP connections with extreme idle time and trivial payload.
Front the application with a buffering reverse proxy or CDN to absorb slow clients.

label: FTP-BruteForce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 21/FTP Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS). Minimum forward segment size: 40 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 2000000.0 (>p99, z=19295198.9); Flow IAT Mean: 1.0 (unusually low, z=-3.8).

output.threat_description: Single-PSH zero-payload micro-flow on FTP/21 with an extreme computed packet rate (~2M pkts/s implied by sub-microsecond duration) — characteristic of a fragmented capture from an automated FTP credential brute-force tool (e.g., Hydra, Medusa, Ncrack) firing back-to-back authentication attempts using the same source TCP stack (window 26883 B).
output.rationale: FTP commonly transmits credentials in cleartext and many deployments lack lockout; sustained brute force frequently succeeds against weak passwords, granting file access or upload capability.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the firewall and enable per-source connection rate limits on TCP/21.
Enable account lockout / fail2ban on the FTP service and require strong unique passwords or key-based auth.
Replace plain FTP with SFTP/FTPS and restrict access to known management IPs or VPN clients.

label: SSH-Bruteforce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 22/SSH Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1, URGx1. TCP window: client init window 241 B (unknown OS); server init window 230 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 400000.0 (>p99, z=259657.1).

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK+URG zero-payload micro-flow on SSH/22 with a computed packet rate >400k/s, a fragment from automated SSH credential brute force against the server. The URG flag in conjunction with ACK and no payload is unusual for legitimate SSH and points to a scripted tool, while the high rate confirms automation.
output.rationale: Successful SSH brute force yields shell access and typically full host compromise; even rate-limited attempts at scale can find weak passwords.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP at the firewall and enable fail2ban / sshd MaxAuthTries and LoginGraceTime hardening.
Disable password authentication on SSH in favor of public-key auth and require MFA where possible.
Restrict SSH exposure to a bastion host or VPN-only access and audit auth.log for any successful login from the source IP.

label: FTP-BruteForce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 21/FTP Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS). Minimum forward segment size: 40 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 1000000.0 (>p99, z=9647597.7); Flow IAT Mean: 2.0 (unusually low, z=-3.8).

output.threat_description: Repeat fragment of FTP credential brute force: single-PSH zero-payload micro-flow on TCP/21 with computed packet rate ~1M/s and the same custom client stack fingerprint (window 26883 B). Consistency across flows confirms an automated tool issuing back-to-back authentication attempts.
output.rationale: Sustained automated FTP brute force commonly compromises weak accounts and exposes the filesystem; impact escalates quickly if writable directories exist.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP and enforce account lockout and per-source rate limits on TCP/21.
Require strong passwords or key-based auth and migrate to SFTP/FTPS.
Restrict FTP to internal networks or VPN-authenticated clients.

label: FTP-BruteForce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 21/FTP Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS). Minimum forward segment size: 40 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 1000000.0 (>p99, z=9647597.7); Flow IAT Mean: 2.0 (unusually low, z=-3.8).

output.threat_description: Third instance of the FTP brute-force fingerprint on TCP/21: PSH-only zero-payload micro-flow at ~1M pkts/s. The repetition cements identification as a high-throughput credential-guessing campaign against the FTP server.
output.rationale: Persistent brute force from a consistent source is a direct attempt at credential compromise; without protective controls, success is a matter of time.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP/subnet and deploy fail2ban-style automated banning based on auth failures.
Enforce strong passwords or keys; disable anonymous and unused accounts.
Remove FTP from public exposure where feasible and replace with SFTP behind VPN.

label: SSH-Bruteforce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 22/SSH Volume: 2 packets total (1 forward / 1 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 0.0 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.0 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 0.0 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1, URGx1. TCP window: client init window 241 B (unknown OS); server init window 230 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred; Flow Pkts/s: 333333.333333333 (>p99, z=216380.8).

output.threat_description: Another SSH/22 ACK+URG zero-payload micro-flow with packet rate >300k/s from the same client fingerprint — a fragment of automated SSH credential guessing. The unusual flag combination on rapid micro-flows is inconsistent with interactive SSH usage and signals scripted brute force.
output.rationale: Sustained SSH brute force at this rate is a direct attack on credentials; successful login yields shell access and lateral movement potential.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP and enforce fail2ban with low failure thresholds on SSH.
Disable password auth, enforce key-based auth and MFA, and restrict SSH to bastion or VPN.
Review sshd logs for any successful authentication from the source IP and investigate.

label: SSH-Bruteforce
alert_text: TCP flow on port 22/SSH Volume: 43 packets total (23 forward / 20 backward), 4593 bytes total (1928 B sent, 2665 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 1.38x (balanced). Duration: 376.8 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 9.0 ms, std 19.1 ms, max 93.8 ms. Forward IAT: mean 17.1 ms, max 93.8 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 640 B, std 135.8 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 976 B, std 268.8 B. TCP flags: PSHx1. TCP window: client init window 26883 B (unknown OS); server init window 230 B. Minimum forward segment size: 32 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow Byts/s: 12189.3933965494 (>p95, z=115.7).

output.threat_description: A 43-packet SSH/22 session lasting ~377 ms with balanced bidirectional bytes (~4.6 KB) and high byte rate. This profile matches a full SSH protocol exchange — banner, key-exchange, and one or more authentication attempts — issued by an automated tool (init window 26883 B). The completed handshake distinguishes this from probe fragments and indicates the brute-force tool got far enough to attempt credentials.
output.rationale: A completed SSH handshake plus credential submission is the highest-risk stage of brute force; if the password hits, the attacker gains shell access immediately.
mitigation_steps: Immediately block the source IP, force-rotate any passwords for accounts targeted, and review sshd auth logs for successful logins.
Disable password auth on SSH, require keys + MFA, and restrict SSH to a bastion host.
Hunt the targeted user accounts on the host for unauthorized activity, new SSH keys, or persistence.

label: DDOS attack-HOIC
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 7 packets total (3 forward / 4 backward), 1246 bytes total (311 B sent, 935 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 3.01x (server-dominant). Duration: 1.3 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 0.2 ms, std 0.3 ms, max 0.7 ms. Forward IAT: mean 0.5 ms, max 0.7 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 311 B, std 179.6 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 935 B, std 467.5 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 65535 B (Windows); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow Byts/s: 982649.8423 (>p95, z=55166.7).

output.threat_description: Sub-2-millisecond HTTP/80 burst of 7 packets from a Windows source (init window 65535 B) returning a server-dominant ~1.2 KB response, followed by RST. The very high byte rate, Windows client fingerprint, and rapid PSH-then-RST pattern are characteristic of HOIC (High Orbit Ion Cannon) — a Windows-based DDoS tool that fires concurrent HTTP requests as fast as possible against target URLs using booster scripts.
output.rationale: HOIC traffic at this rate aggregated across the typical attack swarm rapidly saturates web server capacity and bandwidth; the campaign label and fingerprint indicate an active DDoS in progress.
mitigation_steps: Engage upstream DDoS scrubbing and enable WAF rate-limiting, JavaScript challenges, and signature rules for HOIC user-agents and booster URL patterns.
Block the source IP/ASN at the edge and apply geo-fencing if attack geography is concentrated.
Scale web tier capacity and shift the application behind a CDN with anti-DDoS to absorb the flood.

label: DDOS attack-HOIC
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 7 packets total (3 forward / 4 backward), 1219 bytes total (284 B sent, 935 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 3.29x (server-dominant). Duration: 11.3 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1.9 ms, std 4.3 ms, max 10.7 ms. Forward IAT: mean 5.5 ms, max 10.7 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 284 B, std 164.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 935 B, std 467.5 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 65535 B (Windows); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0).

output.threat_description: Another HOIC-pattern flow: 7-packet HTTP/80 burst from a Windows source (window 65535 B) with server-dominant byte ratio (3.29x), PSH+RST+ECE flags, completed in ~11 ms. Repeated identical fingerprint confirms HOIC booster-script activity hammering the web server with high-throughput HTTP requests.
output.rationale: Confirmed HOIC DDoS activity with a recurring Windows-client signature; sustained traffic at scale will overwhelm the targeted web application.
mitigation_steps: Drop traffic matching the attacker fingerprint at the edge and activate upstream DDoS scrubbing immediately.
Apply WAF L7 mitigations including JS challenges, captcha, and per-IP/AS rate limits.
Notify hosting/ISP and prepare incident communications if customer-facing services degrade.

label: DDOS attack-HOIC
alert_text: TCP flow on port 80/HTTP Volume: 2 packets total (2 forward / 0 backward), 0 bytes total (0 B sent, 0 B received). Duration: 1.4 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1.4 ms, std 0.0 ms, max 1.4 ms. Forward IAT: mean 1.4 ms, max 1.4 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 0 B, std 0.0 B. TCP flags: ACKx1. TCP window: client init window 32738 B (unknown OS); server init window: none (server did not respond). Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Zero-byte flow: no data transferred.

output.threat_description: Two-packet ACK-only HTTP/80 fragment of ~1.4 ms from a custom client TCP stack (window 32738 B) with no payload — the teardown remnant of an HOIC flood request among many being sprayed at the web server.
output.rationale: Single fragment has limited individual impact, but the broader HOIC campaign it belongs to drives serious availability degradation.
mitigation_steps: Block the source IP and apply edge rate-limiting on HTTP/80.
Engage DDoS scrubbing services and enable WAF challenge pages to filter automated clients.
Monitor web server saturation and scale capacity dynamically while the attack persists.
